text: The gallbladder is visibly distended and filled with numerous stones, with the outlines of the stones clearly visible due to the lack of bile. The gallbladder appears non-retractable.
The surgeon uses a clip applier through the patient's left upper quadrant, with the liver retractor in place, to secure structures during the robotic cholecystectomy.
The surgeon uses a technique to pull the gallbladder away from the liver, enhancing exposure of the Calot's triangle.
The surgeon attempts to dissect the cystic duct and cystic artery, focusing on separating these structures from the surrounding tissue within the surgical field.
The surgical view provides a clear visualization of the triangle of Calot, with the gallbladder full of stones visible and the cystic duct and artery exposed.
The surgeon continues with the infundibular approach, manipulating the gallbladder to enhance exposure of the cystic duct and artery within the well-visualized Calot's triangle.
The surgeon skeletonizes the cystic duct, carefully removing surrounding tissue to expose the duct's structure within the well-exposed Calot's triangle.
The surgeon separates the cystic artery from surrounding tissue, enhancing the exposure of the anatomical structures within the triangle of Calot.
The surgeon uses a clip applier to retract tissue, enhancing the view of the surgical field.
The surgeon addresses a laceration by taking down the medial side of the gallbladder, carefully separating it from surrounding tissue.

The surgeon exposes the medial side of the gallbladder, enhancing the view of Calot's triangle for safer dissection. The gallbladder is pulled away from the liver, allowing for careful separation of the cystic duct and artery from critical structures like the right hepatic artery and common hepatic duct.
The surgeon identifies an accessory bile duct located close to the gallbladder. By using the open book technique to pull the gallbladder away, the risk of injuring the accessory duct is minimized.
The surgeon has achieved the critical view of safety and proceeds to remove the nearly detached gallbladder from the liver using the robotic instruments.
The surgeon confirms visually that only two structures remain attached to the common bile duct, ensuring a clear view of the anatomical connections.
The surgeon uses a clip applier to secure a clip onto the cystic artery, which is visible within the surgical field.
The surgeon uses a bipolar device on the distal end of the cystic artery and then employs the hook's cut function to divide the artery.
The surgeon visually inspects the surgical field for any signs of bile leakage from the cystic duct or surrounding structures.
The surgeon attempts to reapply a surgical clip that has fallen off, using robotic instruments to secure it back in place without removing it from the surgical field.
The surgeon uses electrocautery to safely divide the cystic duct, separating it from surrounding tissue.
The surgeon places the gallbladder into an endocatch bag for removal.
The surgeon enlarges the fascia at the umbilicus to facilitate the removal of the gallbladder.
